headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: Deputy AG launches new DOJ fraud division targeting 'insane' problem of benefit scams
headlines.domestic: Senate advances sweeping housing bill as affordability dominates midterm debate prep

metadata.daily_do: Check if you're registered to vote at vote.gov — midterms are eight months away and housing will be on every ballot

clips.caption: When Duke accidentally makes the perfect point about government waste
Dr. Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers (and it's beautiful)
Jax discovers the weirdest workplace harassment case ever

script.text: I've been stress-eating chickpeas about federal housing policy since Tuesday, and now I find out the government can't even track whether a daycare center exists before sending them three million dollars.
It's Wednesday, March eleventh. I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. Let's start with what matters today.

Geopolitics — a Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman had no family to claim his remains. Yesterday morning in Nashville, hundreds of strangers showed up to give him full military honors. The VA rep said quote, 'unclaimed' doesn't fit when an entire community shows up.
Damn.
Oh wow.

Economy — Deputy AG Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division. They're targeting everything from fake homeless shelters to nonexistent daycares stealing federal benefits. Two guys from Philadelphia got four million for Minneapolis shelters that didn't exist.
Four million? Jesus.
That's just two dudes with a rental car.
The new policy — any fraud over a thousand and one dollars gets felony prosecution. Quote, 'no fraud too small, no fraud too large.'
A thousand dollars? That seems—
Low. Good.
Domestic — the Senate is moving on comprehensive housing legislation. It's expected to be the main issue in midterm campaigns. The bill addresses affordability but faces significant challenges ahead.
Finally.
Eight months to the midterms and we're just now talking about housing? Classic.
Alright, let's dig into the fraud story because, um, this gets wild pretty quickly.

So Blanche is saying the federal government hands out money like candy. School lunch programs, daycare centers that literally don't exist. The feds get a bill for two point nine million, and they just... send it.
No verification. No site visits. No— look, when I wanted to expand my business, the bank made me prove my kids existed before they'd approve a family loan.
Wait, they made you prove your kids existed?
Birth certificates, social security cards, the whole thing. But apparently if you want four million federal dollars, just say you're helping the homeless and they cut you a check.
Okay but— Duke, that's not how these programs work. There are oversight mechanisms, there's reporting requirements—
Bree. Two guys from Philadelphia. Four million dollars. Fake shelters.
I know, but—
The oversight mechanisms didn't work.
Right, so we fix the mechanisms. We don't throw people in prison for stealing a thousand dollars.
Hold on. The thousand dollar threshold — that's for felony prosecution. It's actually lower than most states.
Because it's federal money. Your tax dollars and mine getting stolen.
But here's what kills me about this approach — we're talking about prosecuting people who steal a thousand dollars while the real money, the billions, that's getting stolen by contractors and—
Wait wait wait. Are we talking about the same four million dollar scam? Because that doesn't sound like small potatoes to me.
Thank you.
No, you're right, that's a big case. I'm talking about the thousand dollar threshold creating a pipeline to prison for people who are probably desperate.
Desperate people don't drive from Philadelphia to Minneapolis to set up fake homeless shelters. That's entrepreneurial fraud.
Entrepreneurial fraud. I'm using that.
But Bree's point about the threshold is worth considering. A thousand dollars — that could be someone falsifying income on a benefits application.
Exactly. And the deterrent effect argument — I mean, we have decades of research showing that certainty of punishment matters way more than severity.
Okay, but if there's no certainty and no severity, what's the deterrent? The honor system?
No, you fix the— look, the problem isn't that we're not harsh enough. The problem is that we don't catch people until they've stolen millions.
So we catch them at a thousand.
With better oversight systems, not with—
The oversight systems failed. We just established that.
Alright, I think we're circling the same point here. The question is whether this new division can actually deliver on both— better oversight and meaningful prosecution.
Can I just say that quote about bringing up Medicaid fraud at cocktail parties was perfect? Like, yeah, nobody wants to hear about that next to the shrimp cocktail.
But maybe they should. Maybe if people knew how much of their money was getting stolen—
They'd demand better systems instead of just more prisons.
Or both.
We'll see how it plays out. Professor, this feels like there's some historical context we're missing here.
Well, Leo, the creation of specialized federal law enforcement divisions is actually a fascinating evolution. But I want to talk about something else entirely from today's news.
This Navy veteran, Lonnie Wayman. The ceremony in Nashville yesterday. It brings to mind the most sacred tradition in American military culture — the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier.
Oh, that's beautiful, Doc.
The concept emerged from the Great War. By 1921, every Allied nation was grappling with the same problem — thousands of unidentified remains. Mothers who would never know if their sons came home.

Britain buried their Unknown Warrior in Westminster Abbey. France placed theirs beneath the Arc de Triomphe. America chose Arlington. But here's what's remarkable — in every case, the selection process was identical.
How do you choose an unknown soldier?
Very carefully. For America's first Unknown, they brought four identical caskets to the city hall in Chalons-sur-Marne. Army Sergeant Edward Younger was blindfolded and given a spray of white roses.
He walked around the caskets three times, then placed the roses on one. That man became every American mother's son. Every father's boy. Every family's missing piece.
Damn.
The inscription reads, 'Here rests in honored glory an American soldier known but to God.' But here's the thing — he wasn't unknown to God alone. He was known to everyone.
Because when a nation says we will not let you be forgotten, when strangers become family, when community becomes kinship — that's not unknown. That's claimed.
The VA rep said the same thing yesterday. That 'unclaimed' was incorrect.

Precisely. Lonnie Wayman had no blood family, but he had something else — he had a nation that remembered its promise. Every democracy faces this test, Leo. How we treat our forgotten reveals who we are.
That's— I wasn't expecting to get emotional about this story, but here we are.
Yeah, I'm, uh, I'm good with that being the high point of the show.
The guys who showed up yesterday — they didn't know Lonnie Wayman. But they knew what he represented.
Exactly, Duke. Service creates a bond that transcends individual identity. It's why we guard the Tomb twenty-four hours a day, three hundred sixty-five days a year. Even in hurricanes.
Professor, thank you for that. That's— that's exactly the context we needed.
Alright, let's, um, let's bring the energy back up because the weird news this week is particularly weird. Starting with workplace harassment in Ireland.
A boss repeatedly shouted the word 'potato' at his Irish employee. Just... 'potato.' Over and over. The employee filed a harassment complaint.
What the hell?
That's... that's just racist. Like, that's textbook workplace harassment.
Right? But imagine being in HR trying to write up that incident report. 'Employee was repeatedly... potato-ed.'
I can't believe I have to say this, but don't yell 'potato' at your Irish coworkers.
That's good life advice in general.
Speaking of things that shouldn't happen at work — Louisiana classrooms have until Friday to display the Ten Commandments. This is the deadline.
Wait, this is still happening? I thought the courts—
Nope, still happening. Every public school classroom. By Friday.
I mean, 'thou shalt not steal' is pretty relevant given our fraud conversation.
Ha! Fair point. But my favorite story is about Palantir, the AI company. They deployed their advanced weapons detection system... to find office chairs for government employees.
I'm sorry, what?
Military-grade AI technology, designed to identify threats on battlefields, was used to locate ergonomic office furniture. I can't make this up.
That's the most government thing I've ever heard.
I mean, is this related to the fraud thing? Because spending military AI money on office chairs feels—
Oh no no no, this is legitimate. They just used a missile-targeting system to find someone's desk chair. Peak efficiency.
During World War Two, we used advanced radar technology to track German submarines. Now we use it to find parking spots at the mall.
Professor gets it! And finally, Florida man news — a drug dealer locked someone in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt.
Months?
Months. The guy survived on energy drinks and gas station snacks. The storage facility manager finally found him during a routine inspection.
That's horrifying.
That's kidnapping and false imprisonment. That's not funny.
You're absolutely right. I should have led with the office chair thing.
Yeah, let's stick with government inefficiency jokes.
Alright, before we go — your Daily Do. With housing dominating midterm campaigns, now is the time to make sure you're registered to vote.
Go to vote dot gov. Check your registration status. Update your address if you've moved. The primaries start in five months, general election in eight.
Whether you care about fraud prevention, housing policy, or veteran services — it's all on the ballot. Five minutes online today could matter in November.
And check your local ballot too. Housing policy happens at the city level — zoning, development permits, rent control. That's where your vote has the most direct impact.
School board elections too. If you care about Ten Commandments in classrooms, that's decided locally.
Good points. Vote dot gov. Do it today.
Before we close, I'll leave you with this. Winston Churchill once said, 'A nation that forgets its defenders will itself be forgotten.' Yesterday in Nashville, America remembered.

That's The Friction for Wednesday, March eleventh. I'm Jax, Leo's Leo, Duke's still trying to figure out the office chair thing, Bree's already researching housing policy for tomorrow, and Professor Pringle just made us all better humans.
We'll see you tomorrow. And hey — if you're gonna yell at your coworkers, maybe stick to their actual names instead of root vegetables.
